title: Iran conflict disrupts WHO aid shipments routed through Dubai logistics hub - Arabian Business: Latest News
summary: Priority shipments affected include medical supplies planned for Al Arish in Egypt to support the Gaza response, as well as deliveries destined for Lebanon and Afghanistan
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Temporary airspace restrictions across parts of the Middle East have disrupted the movement of humanitarian medical supplies from the World Health Organisation’s (WHO) logistics hub in Dubai, delaying emergency shipments intended for crisis zones around the world, the UN agency said.

The WHO said more than 50 emergency supply requests are currently affected, creating significant backlogs in deliveries intended to support health responses across 25 countries.

The disruption comes as the conflict involving Iran enters its second week and begins to affect humanitarian logistics networks beyond the immediate conflict areas.

Priority shipments affected include medical supplies planned for Al Arish in Egypt to support the Gaza response, as well as deliveries destined for Lebanon and Afghanistan, the WHO said.

The first delayed shipment, containing cholera response supplies for Mozambique, is expected to depart from the Dubai hub in the coming week.

Dubai hosts one of the WHO’s main global logistics centres, which distributes emergency medical supplies to humanitarian operations worldwide.

Attacks on health care and rising casualties

The WHO said the conflict is also placing increasing strain on health systems across the region as casualties rise and health facilities come under attack.

Since February 28, the agency has verified 18 attacks on health care in Iran that resulted in eight deaths among health workers.

Over the same period in Lebanon, 25 attacks on health care have caused 16 deaths and 29 injuries.

“These attacks not only cost lives but deprive communities of care when they need it most,” the WHO said, adding that health workers, patients and medical facilities must be protected under international humanitarian law.

According to national health authorities, more than 1,300 people have been killed and about 9,000 injured in Iran since the escalation began.

In Lebanon, authorities report at least 570 deaths and more than 1,400 injuries, while Israeli authorities report 15 deaths and 2,142 injuries.

Displacement and public health risks rising

The conflict is also creating wider public health risks as displacement increases.

The WHO estimates that more than 100,000 people in Iran have relocated internally due to insecurity, while up to 700,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon.

Many displaced people are sheltering in crowded collective shelters with deteriorating public health conditions and limited access to safe water, sanitation and hygiene.

These conditions increase the risk of respiratory infections, diarrhoeal diseases and other communicable illnesses, particularly among vulnerable populations such as women and children.

Environmental hazards are also emerging as a concern.

In Iran, petroleum fires and smoke from damaged infrastructure have exposed nearby communities to toxic pollutants that could cause breathing problems, eye and skin irritation and contamination of water and food sources, the WHO said.

Hospitals and medical services under pressure

Access to health services is also becoming increasingly constrained.

In Lebanon, 49 primary health care centres and five hospitals have closed following evacuation orders issued by Israel’s military.

In the occupied Palestinian territory, movement restrictions and checkpoint closures are delaying ambulance and mobile clinic access across several areas of the West Bank.

Medical evacuations from Gaza have remained suspended since Feb. 28, while hospitals continue operating under strain amid shortages of medicines, medical supplies and fuel.

The WHO said the escalation is occurring at a time when humanitarian needs in the Eastern Mediterranean Region were already among the highest globally.

Across the region, around 115 million people require humanitarian assistance, nearly half of all people in need worldwide, while humanitarian health emergency appeals remain about 70 per cent underfunded.

The agency called on all parties to protect civilians and health facilities, ensure sustained humanitarian access and pursue de-escalation of the conflict.
